content:
# Exodus Chapter 22 — The EMET Bible (Literal)

**Translator:** EMET | **Source:** Leningrad Codex (B19A), Folios ~45r–45v (PDF pp. 88–89); Codex Vaticanus (Vat.gr.1209), pp. 64–65 (LXX) | **Source Images:** `source-texts/hebrew-OT/EXO_022_page_001.jpg`, `EXO_022_page_002.jpg`; `source-texts/greek-OT-LXX/EXO_022_vaticanus_page_001.jpg`, `EXO_022_vaticanus_page_002.jpg`

---

> **MANUSCRIPT OBSERVATION — CONTINUATION OF THE BOOK OF THE COVENANT:** The Leningrad Codex presents Exodus 22 in standard prose column format with full Masoretic vocalization and cantillation. This chapter continues the casuistic legal corpus of the Book of the Covenant (Sefer ha-Berit), moving from the laws of personal injury and liability (ch. 21) into laws governing theft, property damage, and deposit (vv. 1–15), then transitioning into a series of apodictic moral and social commands concerning seduction, sorcery, bestiality, idolatry, treatment of the vulnerable, lending, and consecration (vv. 16–31). A critical versification difference must be noted: the MT's 22:1 corresponds to the LXX's 21:37, and the MT's 22:2–31 corresponds to the LXX's 22:1–30. This one-verse offset persists throughout the chapter. The Vaticanus manuscript (pp. 64–65) presents the LXX rendering in continuous uncial script across three columns per page.

---

## Laws Concerning Theft and Restitution (vv. 1–4)

**1** [21:37 in LXX] If [ki] a man [ish] steals [yignov] an ox [shor] or [o] a sheep [seh] and slaughters it [u-tevakho] or [o] sells it [mekharo], five [khamishah] cattle [baqar] he shall pay [yeshallem] for [takhat] the ox [ha-shor], and four [ve-arba] of the flock [tson] for [takhat] the sheep [ha-seh].

> **Manuscript note:** LC reads כִּי יִגְנֹב־אִישׁ שׁוֹר אוֹ־שֶׂה וּטְבָחוֹ אוֹ מְכָרוֹ חֲמִשָּׁה בָקָר יְשַׁלֵּם תַּחַת הַשּׁוֹר וְאַרְבַּע־צֹאן תַּחַת הַשֶּׂה. **Key terms:** יִגְנֹב (yignov) = qal imperfect of גנב (ganav, "to steal") — theft by stealth, as distinct from robbery by violence. וּטְבָחוֹ (u-tevakho) = "and he slaughters it" — from טבח (tavakh, "to slaughter, butcher"); the slaughtering or selling makes restitution of the animal itself impossible, thus requiring multiplied payment. חֲמִשָּׁה בָקָר...וְאַרְבַּע־צֹאן (khamishah vaqar...ve-arba-tson) = "five cattle...and four of the flock" — the ox's higher restitution (5x vs 4x) reflects its greater economic value as a draft animal. LXX (21:37): ἐὰν δέ τις κλέψῃ μόσχον ἢ πρόβατον καὶ σφάξῃ αὐτὸ ἢ ἀποδῶται, πέντε μόσχους ἀποτείσει ἀντὶ τοῦ μόσχου καὶ τέσσαρα πρόβατα ἀντὶ τοῦ προβάτου — μόσχος ("calf") for shor; πρόβατον ("sheep") for seh.

**2** [22:1 in LXX] If [im] in the breaking-in [ba-makhteret] the thief [ha-gannav] is found [yimmatse] and he is struck [ve-hukkah] and dies [va-met], there is no [ein] bloodguilt [damim] for him [lo].

> **Manuscript note:** LC reads אִם־בַּמַּחְתֶּרֶת יִמָּצֵא הַגַּנָּב וְהֻכָּה וָמֵת אֵין לוֹ דָּמִים. **Key terms:** בַּמַּחְתֶּרֶת (ba-makhteret) = "in the breaking-in, in the tunnel/breach" — from חתר (khatar, "to dig through, break in"); a noun describing the act of tunneling through a mud-brick wall. הַגַּנָּב (ha-gannav) = "the thief" — the intensive form (qattol pattern) indicating a habitual thief. אֵין לוֹ דָּמִים (ein lo damim) = "there is no blood for him" — literally "there are no bloods to him"; the killer bears no blood-guilt. The plural דָּמִים (damim, "bloods") is the standard term for bloodguilt. LXX: ἐὰν δὲ ἐν τῷ διορύγματι εὑρεθῇ ὁ κλέπτης καὶ πληγεὶς ἀποθάνῃ — διόρυγμα ("breach, tunnel") for makhteret.

**3** If [im] the sun [ha-shemesh] has risen [zarkhah] upon him [alav], there is bloodguilt [damim] for him [lo]. He shall surely pay [shallem yeshallem]; if [im] he has nothing [ein lo], then he shall be sold [ve-nimkar] for his theft [bi-gnevato].

> **Manuscript note:** LC reads אִם־זָרְחָה הַשֶּׁמֶשׁ עָלָיו דָּמִים לוֹ שַׁלֵּם יְשַׁלֵּם אִם־אֵין לוֹ וְנִמְכַּר בִּגְנֵבָתוֹ. **Key terms:** זָרְחָה הַשֶּׁמֶשׁ (zarkhah ha-shemesh) = "the sun has risen" — daylight changes the legal calculus; in darkness the homeowner cannot assess the threat, but in daylight lethal force is disproportionate. שַׁלֵּם יְשַׁלֵּם (shallem yeshallem) = infinitive absolute + imperfect of שׁלם — "paying he shall pay" = emphatic certainty of restitution. וְנִמְכַּר בִּגְנֵבָתוֹ (ve-nimkar bi-gnevato) = "and he shall be sold for his theft" — niphal of מכר (makhar, "to sell"); the insolvent thief is sold into debt-servitude to pay restitution. LXX: ἐὰν δὲ ἀνατείλῃ ὁ ἥλιος ἐπ᾽ αὐτῷ, ἔνοχός ἐστιν, ἀνταποθανεῖται — the LXX adds ἀνταποθανεῖται ("he shall die in return"), making the daytime killing a capital offense, stronger than the MT.

**4** If [im] the stolen thing is surely found [himmatse timmatse] in his hand [be-yado] alive [khayyah] — whether ox [mi-shor] or [ad] donkey [khamor] or [ad] sheep [seh] — double [shenayim] he shall pay [yeshallem].

> **Manuscript note:** LC reads אִם־הִמָּצֵא תִמָּצֵא בְיָדוֹ הַגְּנֵבָה מִשּׁוֹר עַד־חֲמוֹר עַד־שֶׂה חַיִּים שְׁנַיִם יְשַׁלֵּם. **Key terms:** הִמָּצֵא תִמָּצֵא (himmatse timmatse) = infinitive absolute (niphal) + imperfect — "being found it is found" = "if it is surely/certainly found." The doubling emphasizes certainty. חַיִּים (khayyim) = "alive" (plural form used adjectivally) — the animal is recovered living, so restitution is only double (not 4x or 5x as in v. 1 where the animal was destroyed). LXX: ἐὰν δὲ καταλειφθῇ καὶ εὑρεθῇ ἐν τῇ χειρὶ αὐτοῦ — καταλείπω ("leave behind, survive") adds a nuance of the animal being found remaining.

---

## Laws Concerning Crop and Field Damage (vv. 5–6)

**5** If [ki] a man [ish] causes to be grazed [yav'er] a field [sadeh] or [o] a vineyard [kerem] and he sends [ve-shillakh] his livestock [et-be'iro] and it grazes [u-vi'er] in the field [bi-sedeh] of another [akher], the best [meytav] of his field [sadehu] and the best [u-meytav] of his vineyard [karmo] he shall pay [yeshallem].

> **Manuscript note:** LC reads כִּי יַבְעֶר־אִישׁ שָׂדֶה אוֹ־כֶרֶם וְשִׁלַּח אֶת־בְּעִירוֹ וּבִעֵר בִּשְׂדֵה אַחֵר מֵיטַב שָׂדֵהוּ וּמֵיטַב כַּרְמוֹ יְשַׁלֵּם. **Key terms:** יַבְעֶר (yav'er) = hiphil imperfect of בער (ba'ar) — here meaning "to cause to graze, to consume (by grazing)." The same root בער can mean "to burn" (v. 6), creating a wordplay. בְּעִירוֹ (be'iro) = "his livestock, his grazing animal" — from the same root. מֵיטַב (meytav) = "the best of" — from יטב (yatav, "to be good"); restitution must be from the offender's best produce, not his worst. LXX: ἐὰν δὲ καταβοσκήσῃ τις ἀγρόν — καταβόσκω ("graze down") for yav'er; renders the agricultural context clearly.

**6** If [ki] fire [esh] goes out [tetse] and finds [u-matse'ah] thorns [qotsim] and a stack of grain [gadish] is consumed [ve-ne'ekhal] or [o] the standing grain [ha-qamah] or [o] the field [ha-sadeh], the one who kindled [ham-mav'ir] the burning [et-ha-be'erah] shall surely pay [shallem yeshallem].

> **Manuscript note:** LC reads כִּי־תֵצֵא אֵשׁ וּמָצְאָה קֹצִים וְנֶאֱכַל גָּדִישׁ אוֹ הַקָּמָה אוֹ הַשָּׂדֶה שַׁלֵּם יְשַׁלֵּם הַמַּבְעִר אֶת־הַבְּעֵרָה. **Key terms:** קֹצִים (qotsim) = "thorns, thornbushes" — dry thorns served as kindling and fire spread rapidly through them. גָּדִישׁ (gadish) = "a stack, a shock of grain" — harvested grain stacked in the field. הַקָּמָה (ha-qamah) = "the standing grain" — from קום (qum, "to stand"); unharvested grain still in the field. הַמַּבְעִר אֶת־הַבְּעֵרָה (ham-mav'ir et-ha-be'erah) = "the one who kindles the burning" — the hiphil participle of בער (ba'ar, "to burn") + the noun. Note the wordplay with v. 5 where the same root meant "to graze." LXX: ἐὰν δὲ ἐξελθὸν πῦρ εὕρῃ ἀκάνθας — ἄκανθα ("thorn") for qotsim.

---

## Laws Concerning Deposits and Trust (vv. 7–13)

**7** If [ki] a man [ish] gives [yitten] to his neighbor [el-re'ehu] silver [kesef] or [o] vessels [kelim] to keep [lishmor] and it is stolen [ve-gunnav] from the house [mi-beit] of the man [ha-ish], if [im] the thief [ha-gannav] is found [yimmatse], he shall pay [yeshallem] double [shenayim].

> **Manuscript note:** LC reads כִּי־יִתֵּן אִישׁ אֶל־רֵעֵהוּ כֶּסֶף אוֹ־כֵלִים לִשְׁמֹר וְגֻנַּב מִבֵּית הָאִישׁ אִם־יִמָּצֵא הַגַּנָּב יְשַׁלֵּם שְׁנָיִם. **Key terms:** כֵּלִים (kelim) = "vessels, articles, implements" — from כלי (keli), a broad term for any crafted object of value. לִשְׁמֹר (lishmor) = "to keep, to guard" — qal infinitive of שׁמר (shamar); the deposit creates a duty of safekeeping. שְׁנָיִם (shenayim) = "double, twofold" — the thief pays twice the value. LXX: ἐὰν δέ τις δῷ τῷ πλησίον ἀργύριον ἢ σκεύη φυλάξαι — σκεῦος ("vessel, equipment") for kelim.

**8** If [im] the thief [ha-gannav] is not found [lo yimmatse], then the master [ba'al] of the house [ha-bayit] shall be brought near [ve-niqrav] to God [el-ha-Elohim] [to determine] whether [im] he did not [lo] send [shalakh] his hand [yado] against the property [bi-melekhet] of his neighbor [re'ehu].

> **Manuscript note:** LC reads אִם־לֹא יִמָּצֵא הַגַּנָּב וְנִקְרַב בַּעַל־הַבַּיִת אֶל־הָאֱלֹהִים אִם־לֹא שָׁלַח יָדוֹ בִּמְלֶאכֶת רֵעֵהוּ. **Key terms:** וְנִקְרַב (ve-niqrav) = niphal perfect of קרב (qarav, "to draw near, approach") — "he shall be brought near." A forensic/cultic act of presenting oneself before the divine tribunal. הָאֱלֹהִים (ha-Elohim) = "God" or "the judges" — the term is ambiguous; it may refer to God directly (an oath before YHWH), to divinely appointed judges, or to a sanctuary ordeal. בִּמְלֶאכֶת (bi-melekhet) = "against the property/work of" — from מְלָאכָה (melakhah, "work, occupation, property"). LXX: προσελεύσεται ὁ κύριος τῆς οἰκίας ἐνώπιον τοῦ θεοῦ — ἐνώπιον τοῦ θεοῦ ("before God") for el-ha-Elohim, taking it as the divine presence.

**9** For [al] every [kol] matter [devar] of transgression [pesha] — for [al] an ox [shor], for [al] a donkey [khamor], for [al] a sheep [seh], for [al] a garment [salmah], for [al] every [kol] lost thing [avedah] of which [asher] one says [yomar], "This [ki-hu] [is] it [zeh]" — the matter [devar] of both [sheneihem] shall come [yavo] to [ad] God [ha-Elohim]; he whom [asher] God [Elohim] declares guilty [yarshi'un] shall pay [yeshallem] double [shenayim] to his neighbor [le-re'ehu].

> **Manuscript note:** LC reads עַל־כָּל־דְּבַר־פֶּשַׁע עַל־שׁוֹר עַל־חֲמוֹר עַל־שֶׂה עַל־שַׂלְמָה עַל־כָּל־אֲבֵדָה אֲשֶׁר יֹאמַר כִּי־הוּא זֶה עַד הָאֱלֹהִים יָבֹא דְּבַר־שְׁנֵיהֶם אֲשֶׁר יַרְשִׁיעֻן אֱלֹהִים יְשַׁלֵּם שְׁנַיִם לְרֵעֵהוּ. **Key terms:** פֶּשַׁע (pesha) = "transgression, breach of trust, rebellion" — from פשׁע (pasha, "to rebel, transgress"); the most serious term for sin in Hebrew, implying willful breach. אֲבֵדָה (avedah) = "a lost thing" — from אבד (avad, "to perish, be lost"). יַרְשִׁיעֻן (yarshi'un) = hiphil imperfect of רשׁע (rasha, "to be wicked") with paragogic nun — "they [God/judges] declare guilty." LXX: κατὰ πᾶν ῥητὸν ἀδίκημα — ἀδίκημα ("wrong, injustice") for pesha.

**10** If [ki] a man [ish] gives [yitten] to his neighbor [el-re'ehu] a donkey [khamor] or [o] an ox [shor] or [o] a sheep [seh] or [o] any [kol] beast [behemah] to keep [lishmor], and it dies [va-met] or [o] is injured [nishbar] or [o] is driven away [nishbah] — no one [ein] seeing [ro'eh] —

> **Manuscript note:** LC reads כִּי־יִתֵּן אִישׁ אֶל־רֵעֵהוּ חֲמוֹר אוֹ־שׁוֹר אוֹ־שֶׂה וְכָל־בְּהֵמָה לִשְׁמֹר וּמֵת אוֹ־נִשְׁבַּר אוֹ־נִשְׁבָּה אֵין רֹאֶה. **Key terms:** נִשְׁבַּר (nishbar) = niphal of שׁבר (shavar, "to break") — "is broken/injured." נִשְׁבָּה (nishbah) = niphal of שׁבה (shavah, "to take captive, carry away") — "is driven away, captured" (by raiders or wild animals). אֵין רֹאֶה (ein ro'eh) = "there is no one seeing" — no witness exists. The three scenarios cover death, injury, and loss/theft. LXX: ἐὰν δέ τις δῷ τῷ πλησίον...καὶ τελευτήσῃ ἢ συντριβῇ ἢ αἰχμαλωτευθῇ — αἰχμαλωτεύω ("take captive") for nishbah.

**11** An oath [shevu'at] of YHWH shall be [tihyeh] between [bein] the two of them [sheneihem], whether [im] he did not [lo] send [shalakh] his hand [yado] against the property [bi-melekhet] of his neighbor [re'ehu]; and its owner [be'alav] shall accept [ve-laqakh] [it], and he shall not pay [ve-lo yeshallem].

> **Manuscript note:** LC reads שְׁבֻעַת יְהוָה תִּהְיֶה בֵּין שְׁנֵיהֶם אִם־לֹא שָׁלַח יָדוֹ בִּמְלֶאכֶת רֵעֵהוּ וְלָקַח בְּעָלָיו וְלֹא יְשַׁלֵּם. **Key terms:** שְׁבֻעַת יְהוָה (shevu'at YHWH) = "an oath of YHWH" — from שׁבע (shava, "to swear"); the oath invokes YHWH as witness and guarantor. This is the central judicial mechanism when no human witnesses exist: the keeper swears before YHWH that he did not embezzle the deposit. וְלָקַח בְּעָלָיו (ve-laqakh be'alav) = "and its owner shall take/accept [the oath]" — the owner must accept the sworn testimony. LXX: ὅρκος ἔσται τοῦ θεοῦ ἀνὰ μέσον ἀμφοτέρων — ὅρκος τοῦ θεοῦ ("oath of God") for shevu'at YHWH.

**12** And if [ve-im] it is surely stolen [ganov yigganev] from him [me-immo], he shall pay [yeshallem] to its owner [li-ve'alav].

> **Manuscript note:** LC reads וְאִם־גָּנֹב יִגָּנֵב מֵעִמּוֹ יְשַׁלֵּם לִבְעָלָיו. **Key terms:** גָּנֹב יִגָּנֵב (ganov yigganev) = infinitive absolute (qal) + niphal imperfect of גנב — "stealing it is stolen" = "if it is indeed stolen." The emphatic construction stresses the reality of the theft. If the animal was stolen (not just lost by negligence), the keeper is liable — he should have guarded it more carefully. LXX: ἐὰν δὲ κλαπῇ παρ᾽ αὐτοῦ, ἀποτείσει τῷ κυρίῳ — straightforward rendering.

**13** If [im] it is surely torn [tarof yittaref], let him bring it [yevi'ehu] [as] witness [ed]; the torn [ha-terefah] he shall not pay [lo yeshallem].

> **Manuscript note:** LC reads אִם־טָרֹף יִטָּרֵף יְבִאֵהוּ עֵד הַטְּרֵפָה לֹא יְשַׁלֵּם. **Key terms:** טָרֹף יִטָּרֵף (tarof yittaref) = infinitive absolute (qal) + niphal imperfect of טרף (taraf, "to tear, rend") — "tearing it is torn" = "if it is surely torn [by a wild beast]." יְבִאֵהוּ עֵד (yevi'ehu ed) = "let him bring it [as] witness" — he must produce the remains (mangled carcass) as evidence that a predator attacked. The physical evidence substitutes for a human witness. הַטְּרֵפָה (ha-terefah) = "the torn thing, the carcass torn by beasts" — this becomes the technical halakhic term for non-kosher meat (treif). LXX: ἐὰν δὲ θηριάλωτον γένηται, ἀπάξει αὐτὸν ἐπὶ τὴν θήραν — θηριάλωτος ("caught by wild beasts") for tarof yittaref.

---

## Laws Concerning Borrowed Property (vv. 14–15)

**14** And if [ve-khi] a man [ish] borrows [yish'al] from [me-im] his neighbor [re'ehu] and it is injured [ve-nishbar] or [o] dies [met], its owner [be'alav] not [ein] being with it [immo], he shall surely pay [shallem yeshallem].

> **Manuscript note:** LC reads וְכִי־יִשְׁאַל אִישׁ מֵעִם רֵעֵהוּ וְנִשְׁבַּר אוֹ־מֵת בְּעָלָיו אֵין־עִמּוֹ שַׁלֵּם יְשַׁלֵּם. **Key terms:** יִשְׁאַל (yish'al) = qal imperfect of שׁאל (sha'al, "to ask, borrow, request") — the borrower has a higher duty of care than the unpaid keeper (vv. 10–13), since he benefits from the use of the animal. בְּעָלָיו אֵין־עִמּוֹ (be'alav ein-immo) = "its owner is not with it" — the owner's absence increases the borrower's liability. LXX: ἐὰν δέ τις αἰτήσῃ παρὰ τοῦ πλησίον — αἰτέω ("ask, request") for yish'al.

**15** If [im] its owner [be'alav] is with it [immo], he shall not pay [lo yeshallem]. If [im] it is hired [sakhir], it comes [ba] in its hire [bi-sekharo].

> **Manuscript note:** LC reads אִם־בְּעָלָיו עִמּוֹ לֹא יְשַׁלֵּם אִם־שָׂכִיר הוּא בָּא בִּשְׂכָרוֹ. **Key terms:** שָׂכִיר (sakhir) = "hired, a hired thing" — from שׂכר (sakhar, "to hire"); if the animal was rented rather than borrowed, the loss is covered by the rental fee. בָּא בִּשְׂכָרוֹ (ba bi-sekharo) = "it comes in its hire" — literally "it enters by means of its hire"; the risk was built into the rental price. LXX: ἐὰν δὲ μισθωτὸς ᾖ, ἔσται αὐτῷ ἀντὶ τοῦ μισθοῦ αὐτοῦ — μισθωτός ("hired") for sakhir.

---

## Laws Concerning Seduction (vv. 16–17)

**16** And if [ve-khi] a man [ish] seduces [yefatteh] a virgin [betulah] who [asher] is not [lo] betrothed [orasah] and lies [ve-shakhav] with her [immah], he shall surely pay a bride-price [mahor yimharenah] for her as a wife [lo le-ishah].

> **Manuscript note:** LC reads וְכִי־יְפַתֶּה אִישׁ בְּתוּלָה אֲשֶׁר לֹא־אֹרָשָׂה וְשָׁכַב עִמָּהּ מָהֹר יִמְהָרֶנָּה לּוֹ לְאִשָּׁה. **Key terms:** יְפַתֶּה (yefatteh) = piel imperfect of פתה (patah, "to be simple, to entice, seduce") — "he seduces/entices." The piel is causative: he causes her to be naive/willing. בְּתוּלָה (betulah) = "virgin, young woman of marriageable age." אֲשֶׁר לֹא־אֹרָשָׂה (asher lo-orasah) = "who is not betrothed" — pual of ארשׂ (aras, "to betroth"). מָהֹר יִמְהָרֶנָּה (mahor yimharenah) = infinitive absolute + imperfect of מהר — "paying-bride-price he shall pay-bride-price for her" — emphatic: he must pay the mohar (bride-price). LXX: ἐὰν δὲ ἀπατήσῃ τις παρθένον — ἀπατάω ("deceive") for yefatteh.

**17** If [im] her father [aviha] utterly refuses [ma'en yema'en] to give her [le-tittah] to him [lo], silver [kesef] he shall weigh out [yishqol] according to the bride-price [ke-mohar] of virgins [ha-betulot].

> **Manuscript note:** LC reads אִם־מָאֵן יְמָאֵן אָבִיהָ לְתִתָּהּ לוֹ כֶּסֶף יִשְׁקֹל כְּמֹהַר הַבְּתוּלֹת. **Key terms:** מָאֵן יְמָאֵן (ma'en yema'en) = infinitive absolute + piel imperfect of מאן (ma'an, "to refuse") — "refusing he refuses" = "he utterly refuses." The father retains the right of refusal. כְּמֹהַר הַבְּתוּלֹת (ke-mohar ha-betulot) = "according to the bride-price of virgins" — even without the marriage, the financial penalty stands. LXX: ἐὰν δὲ ἀνανεύων ἀνανεύσῃ — ἀνανεύω ("refuse, shake the head") preserves the infinitive absolute doubling.

---

## Apodictic Laws: Capital Offenses and Exclusive Worship (vv. 18–20)

**18** A sorceress [mekhashefah] you shall not [lo] let live [tekhayyeh].

> **Manuscript note:** LC reads מְכַשֵּׁפָה לֹא תְחַיֶּה. **Key terms:** מְכַשֵּׁפָה (mekhashefah) = "a sorceress, a woman who practices sorcery" — piel participle feminine of כשׁף (kashaf, "to practice sorcery/magic"). The feminine form is used, though the prohibition extends to both sexes (cf. Deut 18:10). תְחַיֶּה (tekhayyeh) = piel imperfect of חיה (khayah, "to live") — "you shall [not] let live, keep alive." LXX: φαρμακοὺς οὐ περιποιήσετε — φαρμακός ("sorcerer/poisoner") in the masculine plural, broadening the scope.

**19** Anyone [kol] who lies [shokhev] with [im] a beast [behemah] shall be surely put to death [mot yumat].

> **Manuscript note:** LC reads כָּל־שֹׁכֵב עִם־בְּהֵמָה מוֹת יוּמָת. **Key terms:** שֹׁכֵב (shokhev) = qal active participle of שׁכב (shakhav, "to lie down") — "one who lies with." מוֹת יוּמָת (mot yumat) = infinitive absolute + hophal imperfect of מות (mut, "to die") — "dying he shall be put to death" = "he shall surely be put to death." The hophal indicates execution by others. LXX: πᾶν κοιμώμενον μετὰ κτήνους θανάτῳ ἀποκτενεῖτε αὐτούς — θανάτῳ ἀποκτείνω ("kill with death") for mot yumat.

**20** The one who sacrifices [ha-zoveakh] to gods [la-elohim] shall be devoted to destruction [yokhoram] — except [bilti] to YHWH [la-YHWH] alone [le-vaddo].

> **Manuscript note:** LC reads זֹבֵחַ לָאֱלֹהִים יָחֳרָם בִּלְתִּי לַיהוָה לְבַדּוֹ. **Key terms:** זֹבֵחַ (zoveakh) = qal participle of זבח (zavakh, "to sacrifice, slaughter [ritually]") — "one who sacrifices." לָאֱלֹהִים (la-elohim) = "to gods" — without the article, elohim here means "gods" (pagan deities). יָחֳרָם (yokhoram) = hophal imperfect of חרם (kharam, "to devote to destruction, to ban") — "he shall be devoted to destruction." The kherem is total destruction, the most severe penalty, applied to that which competes with YHWH for worship. בִּלְתִּי...לְבַדּוֹ (bilti...le-vaddo) = "except...alone" — the sole exception: sacrifice to YHWH exclusively. LXX: ὁ θυσιάζων θεοῖς θανάτῳ ὀλεθρευθήσεται, πλὴν κυρίῳ μόνῳ — ὀλεθρεύω ("destroy utterly") for yokhoram; κύριος ("Lord") for YHWH.

---

## Laws Protecting the Vulnerable (vv. 21–24)

**21** And a sojourner [ve-ger] you shall not [lo] wrong [toneh] and you shall not [ve-lo] oppress him [tilkhatsenu], for [ki] sojourners [gerim] you were [heyitem] in the land [be-erets] of Egypt [Mitsrayim].

> **Manuscript note:** LC reads וְגֵר לֹא־תוֹנֶה וְלֹא תִלְחָצֶנּוּ כִּי־גֵרִים הֱיִיתֶם בְּאֶרֶץ מִצְרָיִם. **Key terms:** גֵר (ger) = "sojourner, resident alien" — from גור (gur, "to sojourn, dwell as a stranger"). The ger is a non-Israelite living within the community, lacking the protections of clan and kinship. תוֹנֶה (toneh) = hiphil imperfect of ינה (yanah, "to oppress, wrong, mistreat") — financial or verbal exploitation. תִלְחָצֶנּוּ (tilkhatsenu) = qal imperfect of לחץ (lakhats, "to press, squeeze, oppress") — physical or economic pressure. The motivation clause — "for sojourners you were in Egypt" — grounds the ethic in Israel's own experience. LXX: καὶ προσήλυτον οὐ κακώσετε — προσήλυτος ("proselyte, newcomer") for ger.

**22** Any [kol] widow [almanah] or [ve] orphan [yatom] you shall not [lo] afflict [te'annun].

> **Manuscript note:** LC reads כָּל־אַלְמָנָה וְיָתוֹם לֹא תְעַנּוּן. **Key terms:** אַלְמָנָה (almanah) = "widow" — from אלם (alam, "to be silent, bound"); the woman silenced by loss, without a legal voice. יָתוֹם (yatom) = "orphan, fatherless" — one without a father to advocate for him in the legal assembly. תְעַנּוּן (te'annun) = piel imperfect 2mp of ענה (anah, "to afflict, oppress, humble") — "you shall [not] afflict." LXX: πᾶσαν χήραν καὶ ὀρφανὸν οὐ κακώσετε — κακόω ("mistreat") for te'annun.

**23** If [im] you surely afflict him [anneh te'anneh] [and] if [im] he surely cries out [tsa'oq yits'aq] to me [elai], I will surely hear [shamo'a eshma] his cry [tse'aqato].

> **Manuscript note:** LC reads אִם־עַנֵּה תְעַנֶּה אֹתוֹ כִּי אִם־צָעֹק יִצְעַק אֵלַי שָׁמֹעַ אֶשְׁמַע צַעֲקָתוֹ. **Key terms:** Three consecutive infinitive absolute constructions: עַנֵּה תְעַנֶּה (anneh te'anneh) = "afflicting you afflict" = "if you surely afflict"; צָעֹק יִצְעַק (tsa'oq yits'aq) = "crying out he cries out" = "he surely cries out"; שָׁמֹעַ אֶשְׁמַע (shamo'a eshma) = "hearing I will hear" = "I will surely hear." The triple emphatic construction creates an escalating chain: your certain oppression triggers his certain cry triggers my certain response. YHWH speaks in first person — the law code shifts from judicial ruling to divine threat. LXX: ἐὰν δὲ κακίᾳ κακώσητε αὐτούς καὶ κεκράξαντες καταβοήσωσι πρός με, ἀκοῇ εἰσακούσομαι τῆς φωνῆς αὐτῶν — preserves the emphatic doubling with cognate dative constructions.

**24** And my anger [ve-kharah appi] shall burn [ve-kharah], and I will kill [ve-haragti] you [etkhem] with the sword [ba-kherev], and your wives [nesheikhem] shall be [ve-hayu] widows [almanot] and your sons [u-veneikhem] shall be orphans [yetomim].

> **Manuscript note:** LC reads וְחָרָה אַפִּי וְהָרַגְתִּי אֶתְכֶם בֶּחָרֶב וְהָיוּ נְשֵׁיכֶם אַלְמָנוֹת וּבְנֵיכֶם יְתֹמִים. **Key terms:** וְחָרָה אַפִּי (ve-kharah appi) = "and my nose/anger shall burn" — from חרה (kharah, "to burn, be kindled") + אַף (af, "nose, anger"); divine wrath depicted as flaring nostrils. בֶּחָרֶב (ba-kherev) = "with the sword" — the instrument of war; YHWH will bring military devastation. The measure-for-measure justice: those who create widows and orphans will have their own wives become widows and their own children become orphans. LXX: καὶ ὀργισθήσομαι θυμῷ καὶ ἀποκτενῶ ὑμᾶς μαχαίρᾳ — μάχαιρα ("sword, knife") for kherev.

---

## Laws Concerning Lending and Firstfruits (vv. 25–31)

**25** If [im] silver [kesef] you lend [talveh] [to] my people [et-ammi], [to] the poor [et-he-ani] who is with you [immakh], you shall not be [lo tihyeh] to him [lo] as a creditor [ke-nosheh]; you shall not lay [lo tesimun] upon him [alav] interest [neshekh].

> **Manuscript note:** LC reads אִם־כֶּסֶף תַּלְוֶה אֶת־עַמִּי אֶת־הֶעָנִי עִמָּךְ לֹא־תִהְיֶה לוֹ כְּנֹשֶׁה לֹא־תְשִׂימוּן עָלָיו נֶשֶׁךְ. **Key terms:** תַּלְוֶה (talveh) = hiphil imperfect of לוה (lavah, "to borrow; hiphil: to lend") — "you lend." כְּנֹשֶׁה (ke-nosheh) = "as a creditor, as one who exacts" — from נשׁה (nashah, "to lend at interest, to exact, to be a creditor"). נֶשֶׁךְ (neshekh) = "interest, usury" — from נשׁך (nashakh, "to bite"); interest is a bite that devours the debtor. LXX: ἐὰν δὲ ἀργύριον ἐκδανίσῃς τῷ ἀδελφῷ τῷ πενιχρῷ — ἀδελφός ("brother") for ammi (shifts from "my people" to "brother"); τόκος ("interest, offspring") for neshekh.

**26** If [im] you indeed take as pledge [khavol takhbol] the garment [salmat] of your neighbor [re'ekha], before [ad] the sun [ha-shemesh] goes down [bo] you shall return it [teshivennu] to him [lo].

> **Manuscript note:** LC reads אִם־חָבֹל תַּחְבֹּל שַׂלְמַת רֵעֶךָ עַד־בֹּא הַשֶּׁמֶשׁ תְּשִׁיבֶנּוּ לוֹ. **Key terms:** חָבֹל תַּחְבֹּל (khavol takhbol) = infinitive absolute + qal imperfect of חבל (khaval, "to take as pledge, to bind by a pledge") — "pledging you pledge" = "if you indeed take as pledge." שַׂלְמַת (salmat) = "garment of" — the outer cloak, which for the poor served as both clothing and bedding. עַד־בֹּא הַשֶּׁמֶשׁ (ad-bo ha-shemesh) = "until the coming of the sun" = "before sunset." LXX: ἐὰν δὲ ἐνεχύρασμα ἐνεχυράσῃς τὸ ἱμάτιον τοῦ πλησίον — ἐνεχυράζω ("take as pledge") preserves the infinitive absolute doubling.

**27** For [ki] it [hi] [is] his only [levaddah] covering [khesutoh]; it [hi] [is] his garment [simlato] for his skin [le-oro]. In what [ba-meh] shall he lie down [yishkav]? And it shall be [ve-hayah], when [ki] he cries out [yits'aq] to me [elai], that I will hear [ve-shama'ti], for [ki] gracious [khannun] [am] I [ani].

> **Manuscript note:** LC reads כִּי הִוא כְסוּתוֹ לְבַדָּהּ הִוא שִׂמְלָתוֹ לְעֹרוֹ בַּמֶּה יִשְׁכָּב וְהָיָה כִּי־יִצְעַק אֵלַי וְשָׁמַעְתִּי כִּי־חַנּוּן אָנִי. **Key terms:** כְסוּתוֹ (khesutoh) = "his covering" — from כסה (kasah, "to cover"). שִׂמְלָתוֹ לְעֹרוֹ (simlato le-oro) = "his garment for his skin" — the cloak is his only protection against the night cold. בַּמֶּה יִשְׁכָּב (ba-meh yishkav) = "in what shall he lie down?" — a rhetorical question that pierces the legal abstractness with human pathos. כִּי־חַנּוּן אָנִי (ki-khannun ani) = "for gracious [am] I" — from חנן (khanan, "to be gracious, show favor"); YHWH's own character is the foundation of the law. LXX: ἐλεήμων γάρ εἰμι — ἐλεήμων ("merciful") for khannun.

**28** God [Elohim] you shall not [lo] revile [teqallel], and a leader [ve-nasi] among your people [be-ammekha] you shall not [lo] curse [ta'or].

> **Manuscript note:** LC reads אֱלֹהִים לֹא תְקַלֵּל וְנָשִׂיא בְעַמְּךָ לֹא תָאֹר. **Key terms:** אֱלֹהִים (Elohim) = "God" (or possibly "judges," cf. v. 8) — the ambiguity is ancient. תְקַלֵּל (teqallel) = piel imperfect of קלל (qalal, "to be light, to curse, to treat lightly") — "you shall [not] treat lightly, revile." נָשִׂיא (nasi) = "prince, leader, one lifted up" — from נשׂא (nasa, "to lift, carry, bear"). תָאֹר (ta'or) = qal imperfect of ארר (arar, "to curse") — "you shall [not] curse." Two different verbs for cursing: קלל (diminishing) and ארר (invoking supernatural harm). LXX: θεοὺς οὐ κακολογήσεις καὶ ἄρχοντας τοῦ λαοῦ σου οὐ κακῶς ἐρεῖς — κακολογέω ("speak evil of") for teqallel; ἄρχων ("ruler") for nasi.

**29** Your fullness [mele'atekha] and your outflow [ve-dim'akha] you shall not delay [lo te'akher]. The firstborn [bekhor] of your sons [banekha] you shall give [titten] to me [li].

> **Manuscript note:** LC reads מְלֵאָתְךָ וְדִמְעֲךָ לֹא תְאַחֵר בְּכוֹר בָּנֶיךָ תִּתֶּן־לִּי. **Key terms:** מְלֵאָתְךָ (mele'atekha) = "your fullness" — from מלא (male, "to be full"); refers to the full harvest of grain. וְדִמְעֲךָ (ve-dim'akha) = "and your outflow, your dripping" — from דמע (dama, "to weep, drip"); refers to the juice/oil that flows from pressed grapes or olives. The two terms together = the full range of agricultural produce. לֹא תְאַחֵר (lo te'akher) = piel imperfect of אחר (akhar, "to delay, be late") — "you shall not delay." Offerings must be given promptly. בְּכוֹר בָּנֶיךָ (bekhor banekha) = "the firstborn of your sons" — from בכר (bakhar); the firstborn belongs to YHWH (cf. 13:2). LXX: ἀπαρχὰς ἅλωνος καὶ ληνοῦ σου οὐ καθυστερήσεις — ἀπαρχή ("firstfruits") + ἅλων ("threshing floor") + ληνός ("wine-vat") — the LXX specifies what MT leaves abstract.

**30** So [ken] you shall do [ta'aseh] with your ox [le-shorekha] [and] with your flock [le-tsonekha]: seven [shiv'at] days [yamim] it shall be [yihyeh] with [im] its mother [immo]; on the eighth [ba-yom ha-shemini] day [yom] you shall give it [tittenno] to me [li].

> **Manuscript note:** LC reads כֵּן־תַּעֲשֶׂה לְשֹׁרְךָ לְצֹאנֶךָ שִׁבְעַת יָמִים יִהְיֶה עִם־אִמּוֹ בַּיּוֹם הַשְּׁמִינִי תִּתְּנוֹ־לִי. **Key terms:** שִׁבְעַת יָמִים (shiv'at yamim) = "seven days" — the newborn must remain with its mother for a complete week, echoing the seven-day creation pattern. בַּיּוֹם הַשְּׁמִינִי (ba-yom ha-shemini) = "on the eighth day" — the same day as circumcision (Lev 12:3); the eighth day marks the transition from the natural to the consecrated. LXX: οὕτως ποιήσεις τὸν μόσχον σου καὶ τὸ πρόβατόν σου — μόσχος ("calf") for shor; πρόβατον ("sheep") for tson.

**31** And men [ve-anshei] of holiness [qodesh] you shall be [tihyun] to me [li], and flesh [u-vasar] in the field [ba-sadeh] [that is] torn [terefah] you shall not eat [lo tokhelu]; to the dog [la-kelev] you shall cast [tashlikhun] it [oto].

> **Manuscript note:** LC reads וְאַנְשֵׁי־קֹדֶשׁ תִּהְיוּן לִי וּבָשָׂר בַּשָּׂדֶה טְרֵפָה לֹא תֹאכֵלוּ לַכֶּלֶב תַּשְׁלִכוּן אֹתוֹ. **Key terms:** וְאַנְשֵׁי־קֹדֶשׁ (ve-anshei-qodesh) = "and men of holiness" — not "holy men" but "men belonging to holiness"; holiness is the defining characteristic of the people's identity. The construct chain makes holiness the governing noun. טְרֵפָה (terefah) = "torn, mangled [by beasts]" — the same term from v. 13; what was legal evidence there becomes dietary prohibition here. לַכֶּלֶב תַּשְׁלִכוּן (la-kelev tashlikhun) = "to the dog you shall cast it" — the torn carcass is unfit for a holy people but may be given to dogs. The chapter ends by returning to the theme of consecration: Israel's entire life — economic, social, and dietary — is ordered by their identity as YHWH's holy people. LXX: καὶ ἄνδρες ἅγιοι ἔσεσθέ μοι· καὶ κρέας θηριάλωτον οὐκ ἔδεσθε, τῷ κυνὶ ἀπορρίψατε αὐτό — ἅγιος ("holy") for qodesh; θηριάλωτος ("caught by wild beasts") for terefah.

---

## Translator's Afterword

Exodus 22 continues the Book of the Covenant's casuistic legal corpus, but undergoes a profound tonal shift midway through. Verses 1-15 present classic case law — theft restitution, deposit liability, borrowing rules — with the dispassionate "if...then" structure common to ancient Near Eastern legal collections. Beginning at verse 16, however, the chapter transitions into apodictic commands that increasingly bear the voice of YHWH in the first person, culminating in the divine self-disclosure "for gracious am I" (v. 27).

Key textual observations across the MT-LXX comparison: (1) The one-verse offset between MT and LXX versification (MT 22:1 = LXX 21:37) persists throughout, a consequence of the LXX attaching the theft-restitution law to the end of chapter 21. (2) In v. 3, the LXX adds a death penalty clause (antapothaneitai) for daytime killing of a thief, making the law harsher than the MT's simple "there is bloodguilt for him." (3) The LXX consistently renders ha-Elohim in the judicial oath context (vv. 8-9) as tou theou ("of God"), interpreting the ambiguous term as divine rather than judicial. (4) In v. 20, the LXX renders yokhoram (devoted to destruction) as thanato olethreuthesetai ("shall be destroyed by death"), converting the kherem concept into a straightforward death penalty. (5) In v. 29, the LXX's specificity (aparchas alonos kai lenou, "firstfruits of threshing floor and wine-vat") clarifies the MT's obscure mele'atekha ve-dim'akha ("your fullness and your outflow").

The chapter's deepest theological contribution lies in its grounding of social ethics in divine character. The laws protecting sojourners, widows, and orphans (vv. 21-24) are not merely humanitarian ideals but carry the threat of YHWH's personal intervention — measure-for-measure justice that transforms oppressors' wives into widows and their children into orphans. The prohibition against interest on loans to the poor (v. 25), the requirement to return a pledged garment before nightfall (vv. 26-27), and the final call to be "men of holiness" (v. 31) together establish that economic life is inseparable from sacred identity. In the Book of the Covenant, there is no distinction between the secular and the sacred — all of life is lived before the face of YHWH.
